title: Mainstream Discovery Is the Lagging Indicator Nobody Wants to Acknowledge

source.original: If you want to be early to a trend, you have to go really deep and be an expert – Parker

body_html:
By the time a trend makes it to mainstream financial media, popular podcasts, or trending Twitter topics, it has typically passed through several phases of discovery. Domain experts identified it first. Early-stage investors positioned next. Then the narrative builders—the content creators, analysts, and influencers—packaged it for broader consumption. What arrives in your feed is the final stage, not the first.

This doesn't mean mainstream-discovered trends can't still generate returns. Momentum is real, and trends often run further than anyone expects. But the asymmetric upside—the 10x, 50x, 100x potential—is largely captured before the narrative goes wide. What remains is typically a more efficient, more crowded trade with a very different risk-reward profile than what early movers experienced.

The mechanism behind this is information diffusion. Every layer of translation and amplification strips away nuance and compresses timelines. The original signal—a technical breakthrough, a regulatory shift, a demographic change—gets simplified into a story that's compelling but often misleading about timing, magnitude, or sustainability. Late arrivals are working with degraded information, even if it feels current.

Recognizing this dynamic should fundamentally change how you process trending information. Instead of treating mainstream coverage as a buy signal, treat it as a prompt to assess how late you are. Ask who has been in this space for years, what they're doing now, and whether the easy gains have already been extracted. The answers are often sobering.

The real opportunity isn't in reacting faster to mainstream signals—it's in building the expertise that makes you the source of the signal in the first place. That takes years of focused work in a specific domain. There are no shortcuts, and the people who accept that constraint are the ones who end up positioned before the crowd even knows what to look for.
